# Claims — Range 6 (lines 17906–21092)
# Source: Gamble, Clive. *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (Cambridge UP, 1999)
# Wiki slug: gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
# Prefix: R6-
# Coverage actually read: full slice file through Ch.8 end (~p.426) + opening Notes; body claims drawn from Ch.7 continuation + Ch.8 synthesis.

---

## Claim R6-001
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues that Middle Palaeolithic settlement and raw-material transfers point to gatherings (not social occasions) within lattices of a small spatial framework, with distances between gatherings a maximum of about 5–10 km, so mobility was patterned but local rather than random/opportunistic.  
**Grounding:** "the implication is that Middle Palaeolithic hominids regularly encountered one another within the lattices of a small spatial framework where the distances between gatherings were a maximum of between 5 and 10km."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, gamble-clive, locale, mousterian-industry, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-002
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble contrasts Upper Palaeolithic (e.g. Aurignacian) transfers with Middle Palaeolithic ones: fall-off is no longer simple with distance but directed (provisioning places or focusing on individuals in exchange); larger inter-locale distances, fewer locales, more technological modes on materials moved >20 km; none of these changes environmentally forced, though regional constraints still scale the system.  
**Grounding:** "The Aurignacian, for example, points to a high-density all-channel structure... a directed pattern of transfers... None of these changes was environmentally forced upon the individuals concerned"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, intimate-effective-extended-networks, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-003
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble reconstructs Middle Palaeolithic personal networks as fairly low-density (wheel or star-burst): without letter/telephone-type mechanisms, distance matters for negotiation; paths affirm intimate and effective networks in the normal course of travel; low density is a satisfactory problem-solving solution because information is readily pooled.  
**Grounding:** "the absence of such mechanisms makes the point that in these Middle Palaeolithic systems distance is relevant... These paths led to opportunities of seasonal abundance... Such a network is low density but represents a very satisfactory solution to problem solving because information is readily pooled"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, intimate-effective-extended-networks, mousterian-industry

---

## Claim R6-004
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues Early Upper Palaeolithic movement patterns look more like circular/all-channel networks—linear ‘tramlines’ of raw materials along Rhine and Danube, across Carpathians (southern Poland–Moravia), and Atlantic shells into southwest French locales—less efficient for disseminating information than radial patterns but characteristic of EUP.  
**Grounding:** "this circular, all-channel network seems to characterize the pattern of movement in the EUP. This is seen, in particular, in the very linear movements of raw materials along the Rhine and Danube"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-005
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble rejects reading wheel vs circular networks as two competing society-types (Neanderthal vs Cro-Magnon); rather individuals from both anatomical populations defined systems by different means; after ~33 Kyr bp high-density circular systems predominate and regional archaeological incoherence is reduced.  
**Grounding:** "It was not the case that wheel and circular networks represented two different types of society, Neanderthal and Cré-Magnon, in competition with each other, but rather that individuals from both anatomical populations were defining such systems through different means... Increasingly after 33 Kyr bp the high-density circular systems predominate"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, neanderthals, modern-human-origins-debate, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-006
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble maintains the social landscape does not appear before the Upper Palaeolithic; its construction splices landscapes of habit so agency extends across time and space, has no spatial boundaries in principle (potential global network), and produces increased inter-regional variability under attenuation constraints.  
**Grounding:** "So far in this book I have maintained that the social landscape was not found before the Upper Palaeolithic... the social landscape has no spatial boundaries since wherever people can go, there, potentially, lies the opportunity to construct a global network... increased inter-regional variability in human creativity is a hallmark of the social landscape."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, social-landscape-palaeolithic, agency, global-colonization, release-from-proximity

---

## Claim R6-007
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues the Middle-to-Upper Palaeolithic transition can look like a loss of information-processing efficiency (radial → circular networks); the critical point is chunking information via signs/symbols into externalized codes (visual, verbal, tactile, olfactory), not raw bits—externalization permits representation of action via language or material culture.  
**Grounding:** "it is not bits of information but rather the chunks into which they can be assembled that is significant. To become information these chunks require signs and symbols... In evolutionary terms it is the externalization of information which such codes permit"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, behavioral-modernity, upper-paleolithic-revolution

---

## Claim R6-008
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble restates the complex vs complicated society distinction: complicated society reduces ambiguity via sequences of simple operations referred to external symbolic codes (e.g. bead-making); complex society is performed through the body alone as information source, dealing one-to-one through interaction and embodied gesture.  
**Grounding:** "In a complicated society the individual aims to reduce ambiguity by performing sequences of simple operations. Making beads... is one such example... complex society is performed by the individual through the resources of the body alone."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, agency, presence-and-co-presence

---

## Claim R6-009
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble claims only complicated societies have significant extended networks; power in complex societies relies on embodied affective resources, whereas in complicated societies intimate-network power is also touched by externalized categorization from the extended network, generating ambiguity that can seed ritual.  
**Grounding:** "While both societies have intimate and effective networks, only complicated societies have significant extended networks... power is of a potentially different order... In this ambiguity lies the germ of what we often understand by ritual."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, intimate-effective-extended-networks, agency

---

## Claim R6-010
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim (power thesis)  
**Claim:** Gamble’s power thesis for the transition: Middle Palaeolithic artifacts were embodied extensions of the body (resources for performing society); Upper Palaeolithic power is expressed through conscious play on ambiguity via material representation of the body—artifacts become personified, substitutes for co-presence in social occasions that transform locale into place.  
**Grounding:** "in the Middle Palaeolithic artifacts were embodied as a sequence of gestures... Power in these societies was therefore an expression of these material relations... The difference with the Upper Palaeolithic is that here power was expressed through a conscious play on ambiguity created by the material representation of the body... Artifacts now became personified... a substitute for co-presence"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, gamble-clive, locale, presence-and-co-presence, release-from-proximity, agency

---

## Claim R6-011
**Register:** attributed finding / interpretive  
**Claim:** Gamble notes Upper Palaeolithic type fossils (e.g. Font Robert points) can fix chronological position and assemblage character in a way handaxes cannot; some place regionalization earlier (Bosinski: Middle Palaeolithic; Mellars: Late Middle Palaeolithic possibly after 60 Kyr bp—e.g. MTA triangular bifaces, Altmühlian leaf points).  
**Grounding:** "if you picked up a Font Robert point it would be possible both to define its chronological position with some precision... Bosinski (1982) regards this as a feature of the Middle Palaeolithic, while Mellars (1992:39) places the appearance of such regionalization in the Late Middle Palaeolithic, possibly after 60 Kyr bp."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, mousterian-industry, upper-paleolithic-revolution

---

## Claim R6-012
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble maps Clarke’s assemblage/industry/tradition/culture/technocomplex terms onto network scales: only Upper Palaeolithic gets Culture (regional) and Technocomplex/Social landscape (inter-regional/global, extended networks); Middle Palaeolithic stays at Tradition within landscape of habit.  
**Grounding:** Table 7.4 correspondence of Palaeolithic terms with networks; "I would however subsume the common factors of environment, economy and technology which Clarke puts in his definition as part of the social."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, social-landscape-palaeolithic, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-013
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Gamble estimates Europe’s nine analytical regions at roughly 150,000 to >1,000,000 km² (guideline sizes; sea-level and ice-sheet change affect them).  
**Grounding:** "These give a range of regional sizes (Figure 7.3), from 150,000km² to more than 1,000,000 km²."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-014
**Register:** attributed finding (Siman via Gamble)  
**Claim:** Using C14/TL dates as population/settlement proxies, Siman finds Mediterranean the most densely populated European zone after ~40 Kyr bp; Gamble links this to artifact density/multiple horizons and southwest-region southward population shifts after ~33 Kyr bp.  
**Grounding:** "In her analysis the Mediterranean stands out as the most densely populated area of Europe in the period after 40 Kyr bp... This can also be found in the southwest region, which emphasizes the southward shifts of population... during the period after 33 Kyr bp."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-015
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues Mousterian variants are better described as tradition than culture: MTA has widest French/inter-regional distribution (~800,000 km² over ≥20 Kyr Interpleniglacial); other variants lack single diagnostics; central/eastern groupings have low affinity; only Altmühlian has a weak culture-like claim.  
**Grounding:** "Tradition rather than culture... is indeed the appropriate description... This suggests an inter-regional distribution of c. 800,000km² over a minimum ‘life-span’ of 20 Kyr"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, mousterian-industry, neanderthals

---

## Claim R6-016
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble asserts Middle Palaeolithic can claim a social taxonomy (tradition) but not a social typology (cultures/technocomplexes); style and function were united in making/using gestures; from Upper Palaeolithic, making society is malleable across space/time and inclusion becomes possible alongside exclusion.  
**Grounding:** "within the Middle Palaeolithic there were no cultures or technocomplexes as the terms have been defined above... The finding that life was local, and where the principle of exclusion predominated, is now transformed by the possibility of inclusion through negotiation"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, mousterian-industry, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-017
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble links the MP/UP difference to practical vs discursive consciousness: landscapes of habit host routinized practical consciousness; discursive consciousness makes relational links between domains of intelligence (citing Mithen).  
**Grounding:** "the distinction... between a practical consciousness which leads to routinized action in landscapes of habit and a discursive consciousness which can make relational links between the domains of intelligence (Mithen 1996)."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, social-landscape-palaeolithic, behavioral-modernity

---

## Claim R6-018
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Hahn’s C14 analysis of EUP/late Mousterian (43–30 Kyr bp) yields three median-age divisions: transitional technocomplexes ~39 Kyr (n=18); earliest Aurignacian ~35 Kyr (n=13); Aurignacian I ~31.1 Kyr (n=24).  
**Grounding:** Table 7.6; "Three major divisions are recognized in the EUP (Table 7.6)."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, chatelperronian-industry

---

## Claim R6-019
**Register:** attributed interpretive finding  
**Claim:** Hahn’s isochrons: Late Mousterian, transitional technocomplexes and Early Aurignacian show meridional (N–S) patterning with older eastern dates; Proto-Aurignacian shows zonal pattern indicating rapid movement, with oldest dates (El Castillo, l’Arbreda) in the west—east–west Aurignacian migration not yet proved (Straus).  
**Grounding:** "The Late Mousterian, the transitional technocomplexes and Early Aurignacian all have a meridional (north-south) pattern of isochrons... The contrast with the Proto-Aurignacian is dramatic... Instead of a meridional pattern of isochrons there is a zonal distribution indicating very rapid movement... Straus... reminds us that the east-west migration of the Aurignacian is not yet proved."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-020
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble maps slow meridional spread onto Steele’s Tribal network (resistance to innovation) and rapid zonal Aurignacian onto Poisson network (high transitivity, leapfrogging).  
**Grounding:** "The slow movement can be described by Steele’s (1994) Tribal network... By contrast, in his Poisson network there is high transitivity of ties and leapfrogging."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-021
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Despite supposed EUP advantages, North European Plain proper and much of Italy (30–25 Kyr bp) lack substantial Aurignacian/EUP occupation; Font Robert points cluster in SW France with sparse northward outliers; Germany’s Achtal refitting suggests very ephemeral Gravettian use.  
**Grounding:** "Across this large area no EUP has been found... The Aurignacian is also very rare throughout Italy in the period 30–25 Kyr bp... it might be that in the period 29–21 Kyr bp the valley was visited by a few individuals for a few months."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry

---

## Claim R6-022
**Register:** attributed interpretive finding  
**Claim:** Gamble argues empty/infrequently used central Europe (Rhine–Danube caves with small assemblages; no late Micoquian/Mousterian C14) explains early Aurignacian at Geißenklösterle—people moving into rarely visited hominid space, not dense competition.  
**Grounding:** "This rarely visited, low population density area in the middle of the north-central region may explain the early appearance of the Aurignacian at locales such as the GeifSenklésterle. People were moving into empty hominid space"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, aurignacian-industry, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-023
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Chatelperronian coexists with Aurignacian in SW Europe; associated with Neanderthal remains at St Césaire (Ejop) and Neanderthal bony labyrinth at Arcy-sur-Cure Xb; Harrold attributes Chatelperronian to Neanderthals developing rapidly from local Mousterian in Périgord; Bordes’ long Perigordian tradition generally discarded.  
**Grounding:** "At St Césaire it is associated in level Ejop with an adult Neanderthal skeleton... and at Arcy-sur-Cure, layer Xb, with the very distinctive bony labyrinth from a Neanderthal ear... these assemblages were the handiwork of Neanderthals and developed rapidly from the local Mousterian in the Périgord"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, chatelperronian-industry, neanderthals, aurignacian-industry, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-024
**Register:** attributed interpretive finding  
**Claim:** Early Aurignacian dates in Cantabria/Catalonia (Castillo, l’Arbreda) may indicate Neanderthals left in a Dordogne ‘pocket’ while incomers initially skirted them; co-existence minimally ~43–36 Kyr bp; conditions for imitation/emulation (red ochre, ornaments; Hublin et al. tentative inter-species exchange); St Césaire deliberate burial inconclusive.  
**Grounding:** "they suggest that the Neanderthals were left alone in their Dordogne pocket by incoming people who initially skirted around them... it could minimally have been between 43-36 Kyr bp... Hublin (et al. 1996) go further and tentatively suggest exchange of artifacts between individuals from the two species."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, chatelperronian-industry, neanderthals, aurignacian-industry, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-025
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Arcy-sur-Cure Grotte du Renne level X (Hengelo) has a circular structure with mammoth tusks, post holes, hearths and limestone-plaque bench (~12 m²); Farizy sees major change in habitation-zone perception and lithic significance vs Mousterian levels; St Césaire Ejop spatial analysis shows two disposal zones either side of dripline (~33 m²).  
**Grounding:** "there is a structure with mammoth tusks, post holes and hearths... Analogous changes can be seen in the character of the lithic industries... Backer’s spatial analysis... was able to identify two different disposal zones"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, chatelperronian-industry, locale, neanderthals

---

## Claim R6-026
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble models Chatelperronian as culture-scale polythetic entity (max ~200,000–500,000 km² by time slice) suited to wheel-shaped Neanderthal networks (high mobility, short stays); Aurignacian excels at extension beyond face-to-face society across a social landscape, possibly via male global networks vs female major parenting (asymmetry; Table 7.7 Steele-style model)—no better/worse judgement, but different power construction at wider scale.  
**Grounding:** "By contrast, the Aurignacian appears to excel at extension... Here then is a possible distinction between Neanderthal and Aurignacian societies... Power had a particular context since its source lay in the differential use of the three networks"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, chatelperronian-industry, aurignacian-industry, neanderthals, intimate-effective-extended-networks, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-027
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues Chatelperronian ornaments (pierced teeth, ochre) were imitated/exchanged at encounters enabling extension, yet distinctively Chatelperronian not pale Aurignacian copies; return transmission acted preferentially on males constructing different networks—asymmetry of power from sex-differentiated networks.  
**Grounding:** "the items which are either imitated or exchanged... pierced teeth and the ochre... The ornaments which were produced were, however, distinctively Chatelperronian and not pale imitations of Aurignacian forms... Any asymmetry of power flowed from the different networks now constructed by males and females."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, chatelperronian-industry, agency, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-028
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Zilhão and others: Mousterian persists in southern Iberia long after northern replacement—Caldeirão top of K AMS 27.6±0.6 Kyr bp ends Mousterian; Columbeira similar; Gorham’s Aurignacian ~28 Kyr; Carihuela, Cueva Hora, Zafarraya ~26–30 Kyr with Neanderthal–Mousterian associations.  
**Grounding:** "An AMS date of 27.6+0.6 Kyr bp came from the top of level K which marks the end of the Mousterian... Vega Toscano... puts Carihuela V, Cueva Hora and Zafarraya... in the time bracket 26-30 Kyr bp... Neanderthal remains associated with typical Mousterian industries"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, neanderthals, mousterian-industry, aurignacian-industry, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-029
**Register:** attributed interpretive finding (Ebro frontier)  
**Claim:** Zilhão proposes Ebro as boundary where Aurignacian rapid spread stopped ~38 Kyr bp; Neanderthals continued south until final replacement ~28–30 Kyr bp (favours rapid replacement not long co-existence). Gamble adds Iberia lacks transitional industries and chaine-opératoire imitation of ornaments; denser Mediterranean networks + empty Meseta for EUP; exclusive systems either side of frontier—no need for symbolic boundary signalling.  
**Grounding:** "He proposes that the Ebro formed a boundary up against which the rapid spread of the Aurignacian stopped at 38 Kyr bp... Iberia lacks a transitional industry comparable to the Chatelperronian... Because they were so different there was no need for a demonstration of the limits of the extended networks using symbolic resources"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, neanderthals, aurignacian-industry, mousterian-industry, social-landscape-palaeolithic, modern-human-origins-debate

---

## Claim R6-030
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble uses Iberia to argue recognizing social landscape does not require huts/hearths/art everywhere; stretching space–time is opportunity not inevitability; power as domination/replacement is option not always outcome.  
**Grounding:** "Recognizing the social landscape does not depend upon a glaringly obvious archaeological record of huts, hearths and art... Power, in the negative sense of domination and replacement, is everywhere an option but not always an outcome. The Spanish case study provides a Palaeolithic example."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, social-landscape-palaeolithic, agency

---

## Claim R6-031
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble distinguishes social occasion from gathering: gathering depends on co-present attention and embodied artifacts; social occasion externalizes environment so locales become places via association/rhythms and disembodied symbolic artifacts that structure attention across absence—past organizes future via place–person–object references.  
**Grounding:** "The gathering... depends on nothing more than the attention that occurs in co-presence... The social occasion has any number of artifacts but... many of them are now disembodied gestures... The past is now used to organize the future by making references between place, person and object not only when they are together but also when they are apart."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, locale, presence-and-co-presence, release-from-proximity, palaeolithic-rhythms

---

## Claim R6-032
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov (southern Moravia, NC region) sits on Dyje river under Pavlovské hills (~550 m); ~100 km to Wachau/Willendorf; ~150 km to Moravian Gates/southern Poland; calcareous preservation of bone/organics; excavated ~80 years (Absolon 1924–38; Nazi-period Bohmers stratigraphy; Cold War soil focus; Klima 1950s settlement approach; post-1985 rescue at DV II/III; Milovice 1986).  
**Grounding:** "The landscape of Dolni Véstonice-Pavlov is dominated by the Dyje river and the Pavlovské limestone hills... Systematic excavations began at Dolni Véstonice with Absolon. Between 1924 and 1938..."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-033
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Famous DV finds include 1925 clay female figurine with ‘hood’/slant-cut eyes and 1936 ivory ‘portrait’ head; many clay animal figurine fragments; most Absolon finds destroyed 1945 castle fire; Klima framed great loess sites as permanent settlements with earliest buildings, organized community life (possibly matriarchal).  
**Grounding:** "A female figurine modelled in clay was found in 1925... In 1936 a small ‘portrait’ head carved in ivory... most of Absolon’s finds were destroyed in 1945... 'considerable settlements with the oldest known buildings of man'"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-034
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Svoboda’s Pavlovian/Gravettian chronology: Early Pavlovian 30–27 Kyr bp; Evolved Pavlovian 27–24 Kyr bp (main DV–Pavlov use); Willendorf/Kostenki 24–20 Kyr bp with shouldered points from Don to Moravia/Austria/southern Poland, mammoth-bone houses, storage pits, female/animal carvings. Groningen C14 moving sums: DV II/III west consistently older than classic DV I + Pavlov I.  
**Grounding:** Table 7.8; "the major use of the locale occurred during the three thousand years of the Evolved Pavlovian... Dolni Véstonice II and III, are consistently older than the classic double site of Dolni Véstonice I and Pavlov I."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, kostenki, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-035
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Evolved Pavlovian settlement extends almost 3 km between Pavlov and Dolní Věstonice (200–300 m ASL, two clusters); DV I and Pavlov I (<500 m apart) share occupation phase with thousands of clay figurine fragments and semi-subterranean huts on levelled platforms; DV II has hearths, burials, lithics, bone tools, refitting but fewer fired-clay objects; mammoth-bone middens at DV I/II (human vs natural origin debated).  
**Grounding:** "settlement extended for almost 3 km... Dolni Véstonice I and Pavlov I... have all of these aspects as well as thousands of clay figurine fragments and a series of excavated features... semi-subterranean huts built on specially levelled platforms"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-036
**Register:** attributed comparative finding  
**Claim:** Hearths/kilns are hallmarks of DV–Pavlov and most EUP locales; even simplest hearths usually lacking at earlier MP locales like Mauran (35–45 Kyr ESR) despite near-contemporaneity with Abri Pataud Aurignacian hearths (level 14: five substantial hearths; level 3 Perigordian VI linear cobble hearths ~2 m centres—Binford sleeping model).  
**Grounding:** "it is therefore surprising that at earlier Middle Palaeolithic locales such as Mauran... even the simplest hearth is usually lacking... At the Abri Pataud the Aurignacian hearths are common, very substantial and clearly defined."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, binford-lewis

---

## Claim R6-037
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Lithic densities at Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov open locales are exceptionally high vs earlier open locales (sample of 80): many DV–Pavlov samples >100 pieces/m², none <10/m²; quantifiable increase through time as largest locales shift from gatherings to social occasions; comparable high densities at other NC Gravettian open locales (Predmostí, Willendorf II 9, Milovice) vs small German Gravettian cave assemblages.  
**Grounding:** "Of the eleven samples from Dolni Véstonice-Pavlov, six register more than one hundred pieces per m² and none has a density of less than ten pieces per m²... A quantifiable increase can be seen through time as the largest locales in each period change from being gatherings to social occasions."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-038
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble analyzes Upper Palaeolithic social occasions via attaching and detaching rituals—greetings/farewells always part of intimate/effective networks but now involve performances beyond immediate co-presence so people need not be present to participate/influence action.  
**Grounding:** "These are the rituals of attaching to and detaching from social gatherings... The distinction for the Upper Palaeolithic is that now they involved other performances beyond the immediate attention of individuals. In other words people did not have to be present to participate and to influence action."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, locale, release-from-proximity, presence-and-co-presence, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-039
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** DV–Pavlov ‘setting up home’: platforms 4–5 m diameter cut into loess; post holes and central hearths (DV I); mammoth-bone rings (Milovice); DV II large reoccupied hearths with tent-like posts; Pavlov I possible paired huts; permanence beyond seasonal gathering; mixed ages/sexes (infant plant-food ‘mush’ at DV II; scattered human remains); fibre technology (textile/basketry impressions, possible nets in burnt clay—Adovasio, Soffer, Klima); flint from southern Poland; radiolarites from Slovakia/Austria.  
**Grounding:** "Platforms 4-5m in diameter were cut into the loess... The discovery of what has been tentatively identified as a plant food ‘mush’ for infants at Dolni Véstonice II... textile/basketry impressions and possibly nets preserved in burnt clay... flint transferred from southern Poland"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-040
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble treats long-distance stone and familiar tool styles at Pavlovian sites as social objects symbolizing distant hunting grounds, future encounters, and other social occasions—agency stretched in the social landscape—not mere visual information transmission.  
**Grounding:** "These were more than just good raw materials. They were social objects in their own right... They symbolized distant hunting grounds, future encounters and other social occasions... the act of doing was stretched and extended across time and space."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, social-landscape-palaeolithic, agency, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-041
**Register:** attributed finding / interpretive  
**Claim:** Vandiver et al.: thousands of clay pellets/figurine fragments at DV I and Pavlov I (rare elsewhere); local loess suitable for kiln firing but many pieces show intentional thermal shock—process of making/firing (exploding in kilns) valued over durable product; place-specific skills (loess suitability and kiln location differ between DV I and Pavlov I 500 m apart); not ‘precocious pottery invention’ but loess as active agent in performance.  
**Grounding:** "Thermal shocking was therefore intentional... it was a recognized skill where the process of making and firing was the most important activity rather than achieving a durable final product... it was the way the clay models exploded in the two kilns excavated at Dolni Véstonice I that mattered"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, palaeolithic-rhythms, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-042
**Register:** attributed comparative finding  
**Claim:** Gamble contrasts Neanderthal burial as adieu rather than au revoir with true detaching ritual; Gravettian is key period for European UP burials—few early Aurignacian burials; complete skeletons >30 Kyr (possible exception Combe-Capelle) exclusively Neanderthal; after that open-site and multiple burials appear (Predmostí ‘mass grave’ up to 20 individuals; Brno male with 600+ Dentalium shells, discs, ivory marionette; Sunghir children; Grimaldi tradition).  
**Grounding:** "the evidence for Neanderthal burial had the sense of an adieu rather than an au revoir... The Gravettian is the key period for the study of Upper Palaeolithic burials in Europe... After this date the picture changes dramatically. Burials in open sites are found for the first time. Multiple burials are known of which the largest is the ‘mass grave’ at Predmosti"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, neanderthals, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, upper-paleolithic-revolution

---

## Claim R6-043
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov open-air burial tradition: repeated locale use, multiple burials, extensive ochre/ornament/clothing; ~34 individuals at DV by 1995 plus Pavlov finds; six complete bodies; DV III tightly flexed gracile female under mammoth scapulae; DV XVI male flexed toward hearth with infant mush; triple burial DV XIII–XV (two adolescent males + central pathological individual) with ochre on heads and central pelvis, charcoal/branches briefly fired then smothered; nearby limestone rod possibly lunar-calendar notations (29 marks).  
**Grounding:** "Up to 1995 some thirty-four individuals... have been found at Dolni Véstonice... The triple burial... All three heads were covered in ochre as was the pelvic area of the central body... The reconstruction proposes twenty-nine marks which are interpreted as the notations for a lunar calendar month."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-044
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble reads DV–Pavlov burials as detaching rituals with au revoir character: dead enter extended network of living as ancestors; ochre on head (face/interaction focus) and groin (echoes birth/blood attaching symbolism) partitions temporal continuum; digging (ochre, hut platforms, graves, kiln loess) is primary rhythm of attaching/detaching; permafrost ~80 cm is physical limit beneath surface of fire-transforming action; mammoth scapulae/brushwood as protection.  
**Grounding:** "The Dolni Véstonice-Pavlov burials were rituals of detachment from an enduring social occasion. As such they indeed had the character of an au revoir rather than an adieu... The dead, as ancestors, now formed part of the extended network of the living... Digging was also the primary rhythm"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, intimate-effective-extended-networks, locale, palaeolithic-rhythms, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, ingold-tim

---

## Claim R6-045
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues social occasions are only identifiable within regional social-landscape context (citing Ingold 1993); extension via system integration and externalization in objects is the difference—matched by attaching/detaching rituals and ‘exploding life’ pyrotechnic performance fixing place in memory; earlier periods’ transferable skills vs DV place-specific skills.  
**Grounding:** "Social occasions can only be investigated within the wider context of regional behaviour, here presented as a social landscape... It is the extension of social life through system integration... and in particular its externalization in objects that marks the difference... the performance of exploding life"  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, social-landscape-palaeolithic, locale, ingold-tim, maastricht-belvedere, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-046
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim (Ch.7 summary)  
**Claim:** Ch.7 summary theses: UP regions/locales constructed differently—rhythms now draw on symbolic as well as material/emotional resources; personal networks incorporate intimate, effective, and extended; social occasion and social landscape inseparable, defining locales as places; UP objects exist independent of makers’ bodies and serve system integration beyond face-to-face; previously society limited by effective network, local life, principle of exclusion; extended network becomes power source defining ‘other’ via global counterpart; release from proximity via symbolic + material + emotional resources; social life truly complicated not merely complex; externalizing memory by ascribing power to objects most telling difference.  
**Grounding:** "The objects and artifacts used in the performance of social life now have an existence independent from the bodies of the people who made and used them... they now serve the more complicated role of system integration beyond face-to-face interaction... the ability to externalize memory through the ascription of power to objects remains the most telling."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, release-from-proximity, intimate-effective-extended-networks, social-landscape-palaeolithic, locale, agency, upper-paleolithic-revolution

---

## Claim R6-047
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim / position (Childe rebuttal)  
**Claim:** Opening Ch.8, Gamble rebuts Childe’s judgement (1951:85) that reconstructing Palaeolithic social life is doomed by the data: relative to knowledge of the times, high-quality well-dated European Palaeolithic evidence has always existed for intra-/inter-regional variation; fault lies in approach, not evidence; stopping at 21 Kyr bp is deliberate—later periods get easier (more data, art, burials, recolonization) but must not set expectations that doom earlier analysis.  
**Grounding:** "My main concern has been to demonstrate how Childe’s judgement, still widely followed, that the reconstruction of Palaeolithic social life is doomed by the data was incorrect (1951:85)... If we blame the data for the difficulties of investigating Palaeolithic society then we will always be running to catch up... it has been the way we approach the investigation of Palaeolithic society that has doomed such projects to failure."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, gamble-clive, childe-v-gordon, post-processual-archaeology, processual-archaeology

---

## Claim R6-048
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues recasting Palaeolithic social reconstruction forces rethinking social archaeology of later periods; post-processual critiques already underway (Hodder, Thomas, Tilley); adding Palaeolithic blocks return to social typologies/evolutionist accounts (e.g. Johnson & Earle); aim is beyond calories/tool maintenance and beyond asserting ritual content from graves/caves/heads—beyond idea social life only resided in space between meals.  
**Grounding:** "If... we need to recast the way we approach the reconstruction and understanding of society in the Palaeolithic, then we must necessarily rethink many of the assumptions and approaches to social archaeology in the more recent periods... we will move beyond the idea that their social life only resided in the space between meals."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, post-processual-archaeology, processual-archaeology, gamble-clive

---

## Claim R6-049
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim / quote framework  
**Claim:** Chapter 8 opens with Giddens epigraph—“Human history is created by intentional activities but is not an intended project” (*The Constitution of Society*)—and 1968 Paris slogan “Under the paving stones — the beach”; Gamble’s framework treats intentional acts within socially constituted contexts without teleological colonization projects.  
**Grounding:** Chapter 8 epigraphs: "Human history is created by intentional activities but is not an intended project / Anthony Giddens / The Constitution of Society"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, giddens-anthony, agency, global-colonization

## Claim R6-050
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble’s starting principle (via Wilson): individual as creative but constrained agent, not blank slate or pre-programmed cipher; generalized principle from primate and modern societies returns to individual (not group) as object of society, social life from interaction rules/opportunities and agency as involvement with world/others; abandon search for material remains of rank/religion/bureaucracy; examine co-presence performances with body as prime communication/power, plus changing artifact roles when objects/animals take on traits of people.  
**Grounding:** "I returned to the individual rather than the group as the object of society and based social life upon the opportunities and rules which stem from interaction... This removes the goal of searching for the material remains of social institutions such as rank, religion, economies or bureaucracy."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, agency, presence-and-co-presence, intimate-effective-extended-networks

---

## Claim R6-051
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim (release from proximity)  
**Claim:** Gamble identifies ‘release from proximity’ (Giddens 1984:35; Rodseth et al. 1991:240) as hallmark of human social evolution—extension of social behaviour when objects mediate beyond co-presence; emotional, material, and symbolic resources create bonds of distinct intensity in varied personal networks with recurrent demographic patterned outcomes.  
**Grounding:** "This extension of social behaviour results in the ‘release from proximity’ (Giddens 1984:35, Rodseth et al. 1991:240) which is identified as the hallmark of human social evolution."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, release-from-proximity, giddens-anthony, intimate-effective-extended-networks, behavioral-modernity

---

## Claim R6-052
**Register:** attributed methodological claim  
**Claim:** Against view that individuals are lost in Palaeolithic time: more contact with micro-scale individual action (e.g. Boxgrove piece-plotting) than with faces in Roman Colosseum crowds; Roe’s ‘precious moments in time’; methodologies can tack between fine-grained and coarser space–time scales (Figure 3.2); Clark 1957: inability to identify individuals is no reason to ignore their existence.  
**Grounding:** "There is more contact with the micro-scale world of individual action in the Palaeolithic than there is with the invisible face in the crowd at the Roman Colosseum. Why do we bother to piece-plot thousands of artifacts at Lower Palaeolithic excavations such as Boxgrove if we do not think we can get closer to individual action?"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, boxgrove, agency, locale

---

## Claim R6-053
**Register:** attributed methodological / theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Middle-range theory via Leroi-Gourhan: body as source of action and reference for power; technical acts are simultaneously social acts; phenomenological definition of culture as active engagement with environment rather than cognitive modular linkage (contra Mithen); Dennett’s “nobody home” when looking in brains—brains not main object alone; culture distinguished from society; learning and memory as under-investigated Palaeolithic culture domains.  
**Grounding:** "The key point which I have emphasized continually is that technical acts are at the same time social acts... I preferred a phenomenological definition of culture... ‘the trouble with brains is that when you look in them, you discover that there’s nobody home’ (1991:29)."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, palaeolithic-rhythms, agency, post-processual-archaeology

---

## Claim R6-054
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble revises Binford’s planning-depth contrast (greater among Cro-Magnons for coping with environment): planning depth/anticipation depend on detachment; following Clark and Brody (Athabaskan hunters), planning is social as sharing of memories—decision taken in the doing; taskscape (Ingold) breaks dichotomous ‘games against the environment’ that relegate the social.  
**Grounding:** "I now see this transition differently... Planning depth and anticipation are concepts which depend upon detachment from the world... ‘our planning is as social as our sharing of memories’ (1989:431)... I have also developed Ingold’s notion of the taskscape."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, binford-lewis, ingold-tim, neanderthals, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-055
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Earliest European Palaeolithic societies (500,000+–300,000 ya): late colonization process (not fixed-date event) attributed to social factors; generic Old World face-to-face complex-society skills—attachment/attention rituals; body prime resource; emotional ties require frequent reaffirmation; vertical cultural transmission → faithful gesture copying, redundancy; material resources build effective networks; co-operation requires co-presence; little formal site structure; landscapes of habit not social landscape; life local, principle of exclusion; language as attention device not organizing principle.  
**Grounding:** "I attribute the process of colonization, the late date and the earlier evidence from the Mediterranean... to social factors... The first Europeans had purpose... However, it was never their intention to colonize Europe... The landscape of habit rather than the social landscape contained these people... life was local and the principle of exclusion dominated"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, global-colonization, intimate-effective-extended-networks, social-landscape-palaeolithic, presence-and-co-presence, boxgrove

---

## Claim R6-056
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Neanderthal complex society (300,000–27,000 ya): selective locus in effective network (ambiguity/competition/creativity); after 300 Kyr BP longer raw-material chains of connection across Europe—artifact can signify without seeing gesture, but communication still not means of stretching relations across time/space; skills generic not place-specific (with some movement toward specificity); Levallois/prime-age hunting do not measure rising complexity but latent network properties; no full object–person separation—objects need animation by gesture; vertical transmission reduces options for novel solutions; societies exclusive, local, complex, successful, matched to ice-age rhythms.  
**Grounding:** "After 300,000 BP chains of connection were extended in all regions of Europe... I would contend that in this period there was no separation between object and person... These Neanderthal societies... were, for all that variety and creativity, still exclusive, local and complex."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, neanderthals, mousterian-industry, intimate-effective-extended-networks, release-from-proximity

---

## Claim R6-057
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Transition and complicated Cro-Magnon society (60,000–21,000 ya): stretching relations = release from primate proximity = complicated social life (Strum & Latour); paradoxically record becomes simpler/more understandable (cultures’ time–space dimensions, ornament, places/social occasions, social landscape, objects as people, extended/global networks); reject curtain-raise of civilization or first rung of bands–tribes–chiefs–states; European transition gradual with >15,000 years contact/coexistence in some regions.  
**Grounding:** "The stretching of social relations across time and space, the release from our primate heritage of proximity, marks the development of complicated social life... Paradoxically for any progressive view of change in the Palaeolithic, the archaeological record becomes simpler, more understandable, at this point... The European evidence indicates a gradual transition... over 15,000 years of contact and co-existence"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, release-from-proximity, upper-paleolithic-revolution, behavioral-modernity, neanderthals, modern-human-origins-debate, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-058
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble argues UP symbolic use of materials in extended networks is exaptation foreshadowed in handaxe-making gestures (Gould & Vrba); attaching/detaching rhythms already available for elaborating symbolic ritual; extension requires simpler sequenced action (complication) as price of reduced ambiguity while preserving creativity—two sides of power.  
**Grounding:** "The eventual use of artifacts as symbols, be they handaxes or carved horses, is an exaptation... Extension comes at a price; social life must become complicated, reduced to simpler sequences of actions. Here is another example of the two sides of power."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, release-from-proximity, agency, upper-paleolithic-revolution

---

## Claim R6-059
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** With extended networks, horizontal cultural transmission becomes possible alongside vertical: faithful parent–child reproduction gives way to skipping between networks; culture begins to negotiate people as much as people use culture; asymmetrical power along age, sex, genealogy; life can be exclusive and inclusive via individual’s global network; power as harnessing others to symbolic performance replaces Neanderthal affective/material harnessing.  
**Grounding:** "Instead of learning and memory being transmitted vertically there is now the possibility of horizontal transmission... That curious alchemy takes over where culture begins to negotiate us as much as we use it to moderate our lives... Now life can be both exclusive and inclusive through the extension of social life and the creation of an individual's global network."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, intimate-effective-extended-networks, release-from-proximity, agency, neanderthals

---

## Claim R6-060
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim / synthesis thesis  
**Claim:** Closing synthesis: Gamble does not claim full explanation why extended networks developed late, but argues a social model/method lets research move beyond reconstructions of Palaeolithic people as either stomach-led or brain-dead—“raise the curtain on a much more interesting past.”  
**Grounding:** "with a social perspective we can begin to move beyond reconstructions of Palaeolithic people as either stomach-led or brain-dead. We can raise the curtain on a much more interesting past."  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, gamble-clive, childe-v-gordon, post-processual-archaeology

---

## Claim R6-061
**Register:** attributed finding / theoretical  
**Claim:** Earlier attaching to gatherings (Bilzingsleben; Maastricht-Belvédère) negotiated solely via intimate and effective networks; at Dolní Věstonice–Pavlov, attachment also uses extended-network resources—attention to objects as independent agents structuring action, plus fireplaces and ochre as attention foci linking domestic household to intimate/effective bonds.  
**Grounding:** "Previously, attaching to gatherings had been negotiated solely through the resources of the intimate and effective networks... as at Bilzingsleben (Chapter 4) and Maastricht-Belvédére (Chapter 5)... fireplaces and ochre two further sources of evidence for attention during interaction."  
**Targets:** dolni-vestonice, maastricht-belvedere, intimate-effective-extended-networks, locale, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

## Claim R6-062
**Register:** attributed finding  
**Claim:** Straus (and Gamble) caution: only significant Chatelperronian fossil is St Césaire—we do not know who made early French/Cantabrian Aurignacian, and Mousterian/Chatelperronian/Aurignacian distinctions can be blurred in Spain; still, network (not fossil=tool) framing remains productive.  
**Grounding:** "Since St Césaire is the only significant fossil we have, we do not even know ‘who’ made the early Aurignacian of France, or, for that matter, Cantabrian Spain, where in fact the distinctions among Mousterian, Chatelperronian, and Aurignacian are somewhat blurred"  
**Targets:** chatelperronian-industry, aurignacian-industry, neanderthals, modern-human-origins-debate, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999

---

## Claim R6-063
**Register:** attributed theoretical claim  
**Claim:** Gamble’s Table 7.4 / regional argument: technocomplex term fills need for broad similarity but is problematic when applied to both MP and UP (Typical Mousterian and Gravettian both ‘weak cultures’); landscape of habit better assesses differences while recognizing common evolutionary behavioural core; Typical Mousterian/Gravettian should not be collapsed as purely adaptive technocomplexes.  
**Grounding:** "Hence the Typical Mousterian and the Gravettian could both be regarded as technocomplexes in the sense that they are both ‘weak’ cultures... The landscape of habit avoids this problem"  
**Targets:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999, mousterian-industry, social-landscape-palaeolithic

---

## Claim R6-064
**Register:** attributed comparative finding  
**Claim:** Cantabrian EUP (Straus): mosaic/gradual transition from MP adaptations; slight raw-material transfers, rare portable art/ornaments; Cueva Morín burials as notable difference; yet stone-style links area to wider UP world north of Pyrenees rather than to Ebro-south Neanderthals—supports exclusive juxtaposed systems without symbolic frontier signalling.  
**Grounding:** "The whole EUP in Cantabrian Spain constituted a long, irregular, and, in many domains, gradual transition... The links established through stone artifact styles key this area into that wider Upper Palaeolithic world north of the Pyrenees rather than to the Neanderthals south of the Ebro."  
**Targets:** aurignacian-industry, neanderthals, social-landscape-palaeolithic, modern-human-origins-debate, gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
